title: Artie and Angie and Hank and Hercules

jokes.text: If you're gonna sit around all day with your thumb up your ass, sooner or later that will impact the show.
jokes.setup: Artie lecturing about professionalism and discipline
jokes.punchline: The crude metaphor delivered in his matter-of-fact producer voice
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie - crude wisdom delivered with complete seriousness. The juxtaposition of professional advice with vulgar imagery is character-specific and well-executed.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If you're gonna sit around all day with your thumb up your ass, sooner or later that will impact the show.

jokes.text: I hate when people use their children as an excuse.
jokes.setup: Sigourney Weaver cancels because her kid is sick
jokes.punchline: Larry's callous dismissal of genuine parental concern
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry narcissism - everything revolves around his show. The selfish perspective is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I hate when people use their children as an excuse.

jokes.text: Mary Lou, that's the reason people in Hollywood have kids.
jokes.setup: Larry complaining about using children as excuses
jokes.punchline: Artie's cynical industry insight about strategic procreation
jokes.explanation: Sharp industry satire delivered with perfect Artie deadpan. The cynicism feels earned from his decades in showbiz, and it's highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Mary Lou, that's the reason people in Hollywood have kids.

jokes.text: Boy, I'd like to have that police woman put me in a choke hold. You know, frisk me, baby. Whoa, do I have a concealed weapon for you.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Angie Dickinson appearing on the show
jokes.punchline: Hank's inappropriate sexual fantasizing about her Police Woman character
jokes.explanation: Typical Hank inappropriateness. While perfectly in character, the sexual innuendo feels somewhat predictable and relies on cheap shock value.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Boy, I'd like to have that police woman put me in a choke hold. You know, frisk me, baby. Whoa, do I have a concealed weapon for you.

jokes.text: Shut up, Hank. Officer, officer, turn off that siren and let's fuck.
jokes.setup: Hank's sexual Police Woman fantasy
jokes.punchline: Larry one-upping him with an even more crude and direct fantasy
jokes.explanation: Larry's competitive nature kicks in - he can't let Hank be more inappropriate than him. The escalation works and the economy is good.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Shut up, Hank. Officer, officer, turn off that siren and let's fuck.

jokes.text: You're gonna have to be more specific.
jokes.setup: Artie asks if Larry's ever had a woman scramble his brains with lust
jokes.punchline: Larry's matter-of-fact response implying this happens regularly
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry Sanders moment - the deadpan delivery suggests this is a common occurrence for him. Maximum comedy with minimal words, perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: You're gonna have to be more specific.

jokes.text: Well, we had a lot of hot sex, but we never left the house.
jokes.setup: Larry asks if Artie used to date Angie Dickinson
jokes.punchline: Artie's specific clarification about their relationship parameters
jokes.explanation: Artie's matter-of-fact approach to describing a purely physical relationship. The specificity makes it funny.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Well, we had a lot of hot sex, but we never left the house.

jokes.text: She's like a siren calling for me to smash my nuts upon the rocks.
jokes.setup: Artie explaining Angie's power over him
jokes.punchline: The crude twist on the classical Odyssey reference
jokes.explanation: Brilliant classical reference subverted with crude physicality. Shows Artie's intelligence while maintaining his earthy character. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: She's like a siren calling for me to smash my nuts upon the rocks.

jokes.text: Ha! OK, it's not that different.
jokes.setup: Artie comparing Angie to booze but saying he doesn't know when to stop
jokes.punchline: Larry pointing out the comparison actually holds up perfectly
jokes.explanation: Larry's logical brain cutting through Artie's attempted distinction. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Ha! OK, it's not that different.

jokes.text: Hey, could you insult us?
jokes.setup: Staff members meeting Don Rickles
jokes.punchline: Their eager request to be professionally insulted
jokes.explanation: Perfect show business moment - starstruck fans wanting the full experience even if it means being insulted. The desperation is palpable and funny.

dialogue.line: Hey, could you insult us?

jokes.text: Oh, not really, you know, I don't do that. You know, I do that for, you know, when you get paid.
jokes.setup: Request for free insults
jokes.punchline: Rickles explaining he only does his act for money
jokes.explanation: Meta-commentary on the entertainment industry - even insults are a professional service. Rickles being reasonable about his craft.

dialogue.speaker: Don Rickles
dialogue.line: Oh, not really, you know, I don't do that. You know, I do that for, you know, when you get paid.

jokes.text: You must have come out of your mother on an angle and hit the wall. You got a funny-looking body.
jokes.setup: Staff begging for insults
jokes.punchline: Rickles delivering his trademark brutal physical observations
jokes.explanation: Classic Rickles material - the birth angle joke is original and brutal. Perfectly delivered in character, highly quotable insult comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Don Rickles
dialogue.line: You must have come out of your mother on an angle and hit the wall. You got a funny-looking body.

jokes.text: It's like I shit in Pluto's dish or something.
jokes.setup: Hank explaining his trouble getting work at Disney
jokes.punchline: The absurd metaphor of defiling Disney's dog mascot
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd Hank metaphor. The visual of literally defiling Disney property to explain career troubles is perfectly weird and quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: It's like I shit in Pluto's dish or something.

jokes.text: I knew television was in deep, deep trouble the day they took C.P.O. Sharkey off the air.
jokes.setup: Hank trying to bond with Don Rickles
jokes.punchline: His absurd overvaluation of a mediocre Don Rickles sitcom
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank - trying to kiss up by referencing Rickles' least successful show as a masterpiece. The brown-nosing is painfully obvious and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I knew television was in deep, deep trouble the day they took C.P.O. Sharkey off the air.

jokes.text: What am I, with the Mossad? I don't know.
jokes.setup: Larry asking how to spell 'chazzer'
jokes.punchline: Rickles comparing spelling to Israeli intelligence work
jokes.explanation: Classic Rickles - taking a simple question and making it absurdly dramatic. The Mossad reference is unexpected and perfectly delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Don Rickles
dialogue.line: What am I, with the Mossad? I don't know.

jokes.text: Here's a man that's a nebbish. That means, as you can see, does nothing, just sits there and smells his hand.
jokes.setup: Explaining what a 'nebbish' is
jokes.punchline: The specific visual of Larry apparently smelling his own hand
jokes.explanation: Rickles using Larry as a live example. The hand-smelling detail is specific and visual, making Larry the unwitting prop in the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Don Rickles
dialogue.line: Here's a man that's a nebbish. That means, as you can see, does nothing, just sits there and smells his hand.

jokes.text: Artie, forget about it, huh? You're old. It's over. Go out to the cemetery and pick out a plot.
jokes.setup: Angie complimenting Artie
jokes.punchline: Brutal age-based reality check with death imagery
jokes.explanation: Classic Rickles - the escalation from 'you're old' to 'pick out a cemetery plot' is perfectly brutal and specific.

dialogue.speaker: Angie
dialogue.line: Artie, forget about it, huh? You're old. It's over. Go out to the cemetery and pick out a plot.

jokes.text: Angie kissing Larry unexpectedly
jokes.setup: Larry saying goodbye to Angie after the show
jokes.punchline: Angie suddenly kissing him passionately, catching him off guard
jokes.explanation: Visual comedy showing Larry's discomfort and the awkwardness of the situation. Sets up Artie's later struggle with temptation.

jokes.text: He's just thanking her for doing the show.
jokes.setup: Larry clearly flustered and aroused after Angie kisses him
jokes.punchline: His weak attempt to downplay what just happened
jokes.explanation: Larry's transparent attempt to maintain professionalism while clearly affected. The understatement makes his arousal more obvious.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: He's just thanking her for doing the show.

jokes.text: No, no. Not really. Uh, he'd been on the plane the flight before mine. You could still feel the Eisner in the air.
jokes.setup: Hank claiming he sat next to Michael Eisner on a plane
jokes.punchline: Admitting it's not true but doubling down with the absurd concept of residual Eisner presence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank - turning an exposed lie into an even more absurd claim. The concept of 'feeling the Eisner' is brilliantly weird and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: No, no. Not really. Uh, he'd been on the plane the flight before mine. You could still feel the Eisner in the air.

jokes.text: That's Samson.
jokes.setup: Hank confusing Hercules with the biblical story of hair giving strength
jokes.punchline: The executive's flat correction
jokes.explanation: Simple correction joke showing Hank's cultural illiteracy. The deadpan delivery makes Hank's mistake more obvious.

dialogue.speaker: Disney Executive
dialogue.line: That's Samson.

jokes.text: I don't think so.
jokes.setup: Being corrected about mythological figures
jokes.punchline: Hank's stubborn refusal to accept he's wrong
jokes.explanation: Classic Hank - doubling down when wrong. His confidence in the face of obvious error is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I don't think so.

jokes.text: Is he a gynecologist?
jokes.setup: Discussion of Hercules performing his 12 labors
jokes.punchline: Hank mishearing 'labors' as related to childbirth
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay showing Hank's literal-minded confusion. The pun works on multiple levels and fits his character perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Is he a gynecologist?

jokes.text: It's fine. I'm that way when Siegfried and Roy are on.
jokes.setup: Artie apologizing for pushing Larry out during Angie's visit
jokes.punchline: Larry admitting he has similar reactions to other acts
jokes.explanation: Larry's unexpected confession about being aroused by the Vegas magicians. The specificity makes it funnier and more believable.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: It's fine. I'm that way when Siegfried and Roy are on.

jokes.text: Tomorrow let's switch to beer.
jokes.setup: Angie complaining they're in a rut of drinking wine and having sex
jokes.punchline: Artie's literal solution to only change the beverage
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/punchline structure. Artie completely missing the point of her complaint and offering the most literal solution possible. Highly quotable and character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Tomorrow let's switch to beer.

jokes.text: You know who's really good in this one? David Schwimmer. He's hilarious in this one.
jokes.setup: Watching Melrose Place with Angie
jokes.punchline: Artie claiming David Schwimmer (from Friends) is in the wrong show
jokes.explanation: Artie's confusion between TV shows, or possibly deliberate misdirection. The confidence in the wrong information is funny.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: You know who's really good in this one? David Schwimmer. He's hilarious in this one.

jokes.text: Oh, boy, he's just like Marcel Marceau.
jokes.setup: Watching Larry's show in Italian where he can't understand the words
jokes.punchline: Comparing Larry to a mime, suggesting his comedy is purely physical
jokes.explanation: Artie's unintentionally backhanded compliment - suggesting Larry's comedy works even when you can't understand the words.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh, boy, he's just like Marcel Marceau.

jokes.text: Not on TV.
jokes.setup: Angie pointing out he's already seen this Larry Sanders episode
jokes.punchline: Artie's distinction between seeing it in person vs. broadcast
jokes.explanation: Artie's obsessive producer mentality - he needs to see how it plays on television even though he was there. The distinction is absurd but believable for his character.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Not on TV.

jokes.text: If Dressed to Kill were on right now, you'd be humping the set crying out 'Mama mia!'
jokes.setup: Angie calling his Larry Sanders obsession sick
jokes.punchline: Artie's counterattack about her potential reaction to her own movie
jokes.explanation: Artie fighting back with a vivid image of Angie being equally obsessive about her own work. The Italian exclamation adds flavor to the insult.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If Dressed to Kill were on right now, you'd be humping the set crying out 'Mama mia!'

jokes.text: What are they doing, a live action version of Dumbo? You throw in 40 pounds, a little gray makeup, you're gonna be great.
jokes.setup: Hank excitedly announcing he's playing Hercules
jokes.punchline: Larry's cruel suggestion he'd be better as the elephant
jokes.explanation: Larry's reflexive cruelty toward Hank. The specific details about makeup and weight make it more brutal and visual.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What are they doing, a live action version of Dumbo? You throw in 40 pounds, a little gray makeup, you're gonna be great.

jokes.text: What are they doing, a live action version of Dumbo? You throw in 40 pounds, a little gray makeup, you're gonna be great.
jokes.setup: Hank announcing he got the Disney Hercules role
jokes.punchline: Larry suggesting he'd be better suited to play an elephant
jokes.explanation: Standard fat joke with specific Disney reference. Not particularly original but delivers on character dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What are they doing, a live action version of Dumbo? You throw in 40 pounds, a little gray makeup, you're gonna be great.

jokes.text: This is a warning, my friend. You do not mess with Hercules. I killed my wife, I killed my children, and I shall kill you if you do not temper thy tongue.
jokes.setup: Larry mocking Hank's Disney role
jokes.punchline: Hank going full method actor with mythological threats
jokes.explanation: Hank completely losing himself in the role, becoming genuinely threatening. The escalation from Disney voice work to mythological murder is absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: This is a warning, my friend. You do not mess with Hercules. I killed my wife, I killed my children, and I shall kill you if you do not temper thy tongue.

jokes.text: I don't mean to question your judgment, but one of my balls is as big as Tate Donovan.
jokes.setup: Learning Tate Donovan got the Hercules role instead
jokes.punchline: Hank's crude size comparison to express his superiority
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly crude Hank logic - using testicular size as a measure of acting ability. The specificity makes it quotable and shocking.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: I don't mean to question your judgment, but one of my balls is as big as Tate Donovan.

jokes.text: Hiageus, the village idiot, enters, a sad little stump of a man with a sorry mustache. He's being pelted with stones by a band of toughs. He cries out for Hercules in his funny squeal of a voice.
jokes.setup: Hank reading the character description
jokes.punchline: The brutally accurate description of what amounts to Hank himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect meta-joke where the character description exactly matches Hank's real persona. The 'sorry mustache' detail seals it.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Hiageus, the village idiot, enters, a sad little stump of a man with a sorry mustache. He's being pelted with stones by a band of toughs. He cries out for Hercules in his funny squeal of a voice.

jokes.text: Do you think that when Hiageus gets in trouble, he could say, uh, 'Hey, now!'
jokes.setup: Reconsidering the village idiot role
jokes.punchline: Wanting to incorporate his signature catchphrase into ancient Greece
jokes.explanation: Hank trying to inject his modern persona into ancient mythology. The desperation to maintain his brand identity is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Do you think that when Hiageus gets in trouble, he could say, uh, 'Hey, now!'

jokes.text: And is that your tail I see between your legs?
jokes.setup: Artie returning from Italy looking defeated
jokes.punchline: Beverly's twist on the 'tail between legs' idiom
jokes.explanation: Beverly's dry wit applied to Artie's obvious failure. The literal interpretation of the metaphor works for her character.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: And is that your tail I see between your legs?

jokes.text: I'm just not sure that I'm ready to be in a relationship with someone in show business. Exactly.
jokes.setup: Laura explaining her hesitation about moving in with Larry
jokes.punchline: Her realization that she's also in show business
jokes.explanation: Self-awareness joke about Hollywood hypocrisy. The quick realization and correction makes the logic clear.

dialogue.speaker: Laura
dialogue.line: I'm just not sure that I'm ready to be in a relationship with someone in show business.
Exactly.

jokes.text: We were on vacation, for Christ's sake. You know, it started out good, but then she wanted to talk about my peccadilloes. My mood swings, my temper, my stubbornness. My obsession with Glenlivet.
jokes.setup: Artie explaining why the Italy trip failed
jokes.punchline: The laundry list of his obvious character flaws
jokes.explanation: Artie's complete lack of self-awareness about his obvious flaws. The escalating list and specific Glenlivet detail make it character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: We were on vacation, for Christ's sake. You know, it started out good, but then she wanted to talk about my peccadilloes. My mood swings, my temper, my stubbornness. My obsession with Glenlivet.

jokes.text: What time's your flight? 9:30. I'll be back on Monday. I swear. Thanks, buddy.
jokes.setup: Artie assuring Larry that Angie is leaving for Mexico
jokes.punchline: The implication that Artie is also going to Mexico despite his promises
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to Artie's weakness for Angie. The audience realizes he's going with her before Larry does, creating dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: What time's your flight?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: 9:30. I'll be back on Monday. I swear.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: Thanks, buddy.
